dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 responding for unconscious/fainting at 1234 E Broad Street.
Hi, I'm Taylor, a paramedic with the city EMS. What happened here?
He's not moving. I found him sitting by the stairs like this.
Can you hear me sir? What's your name? (No response)
Eyes aren't opening. He's not responding to voice or touch.
No verbal response.
He doesn't move at all. He feels limp.
He is unconscious. Airway is patent, no active obstruction noted.
No chest rise. Not seeing any spontaneous breathing.
No spontaneous respirations. Initiating BVM for assisted breathing.
No carotid or radial pulse. CPR is needed.
No signs of circulation. Initiating CPR and assisted breathing.
Starting manual CPR to support circulation.
The EKG shows PEA. No organized electrical activity detected.
Blood pressure is 0/0. Not obtainable.
SpO2 is 0. No circulation detected for a reading.
A NPA is being inserted. Head will be tilted back and neck extended.
Giving breaths with the BVM. Oral cavity cleared first.
Oral airway is clear now. Breath sounds heard after each ventilation.
Assisted respirations now at 12 per minute.
Blood sugar is significantly elevated. Diabetic patient likely in hyperglycemia.
Blood glucose reading is 355 mg/dL.
Using a four-person lift to move the patient to the stretcher.
No visible injuries or deformities to the extremities.
No bruises or rashes found during the skin exam.
No obvious head or neck injuries.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: There's an active nosebleed. It started last night. 28. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Paramedic: What was he doing before losing responsiveness? Any symptoms?
Could you tell me his name and any medical conditions?
This is my brother. He's 54 and had insulin-dependent diabetes.
He had breakfast with cereal a while ago.

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: He was fine in the morning. Found him in the same spot like that.
Engaging the cardiac arrest protocol, 2-7, now.
Supporting ventilations using BVM with 15 LPM oxygen.
Suctioning oral cavity and preparing for intubation.
Left humeral intraosseous access is established.
Giving 1mg of 1:10,000 Epi via IO access.
Switching to AutoPulse and confirming rhythm and compressions
EKG now showing asystole. No electrical activity.
Using the AutoPulse as per protocol for compressions.
Transport is going to [COMPANY_NAME] trauma center. 42. Reassessments; redo pain assessment: severity; Paramedic: No signs of pain or discomfort detected in the patient.
Patient is secured for transport. Equipment verified.
All equipment secured. Ambulance is ready for movement.
ROSC achieved at hospital after patient was transferred.
All crew confirmed on board. Transport proceeding.
Continuous cardiac monitoring set and medications prepared for repeat as needed.
SpO2 remains unobtainable due to pulse.
Communications opened with destination - hospital green status with ROSC.
Red light set to high with siren for priority transport. 51. Reassessments; redo history of present illness: pertinent past medical history; Paramedic: Confirming diabetic status with family.
He had breakfast earlier this morning.
He was last seen fine at 9 AM. Found him unresponsive like this.
Re-checking EKG rhythm en route. Still asystole.
Reevaluating airway placement with end tidal CO2 and resecuring ET.

Maintaining autopulse and continuous manual compressions as backup. 57. Reassessments; retake vital signs: spo2; Partner: No changes in SpO2. Still 0 due to pulse. 58. Reassessments; redo history of present illness: pertinent past medical history; Paramedic: No known allergies.

Rechecking blood glucose and remains at 355 mg/dL. 60. Reassessments; redo history of present illness: signs and symptoms; Paramedic: No reported signs or symptoms from patient due to unresponsiveness. 61. Reassessments; redo history of present illness: medications; Paramedic: Current meds are unknown due to availability.
No timeline for pain - patient is unresponsive.
EKG showed loss of ROSC in transport due to crew closest response.
Resuming compressions with AutoPulse and rechecking pulse. 65. Reassessments; retake vital signs: pulse; Partner: Pulse remains unobtainable. Continuing compressions.
Redirecting to receiving hospital for emergent cardio arrest care.
Patient secured with straps fully in line. Transporting now.
Second round of 1mg of Epi prepared per protocol.
Airway secured with suction and monitoring.
Rhythm rechecked. PEA detected with positive compressions.
Turning over to receiving Trauma team green status.
Emergency care complete. Cutting motors.
No specific provocation for current condition. 74. Reassessments; redo pain assessment: quality; Paramedic: Not applicable due to unresponsive state.
Airway reassessed with oxygen and breath sounds clear.

Medication ready for administration if needed within next rounds. 77. Reassessments; retake vital signs: resp; Partner: No ventilation response observed due to critical state. 78. Reassessments; retake vital signs: bp; Partner: Nonsupported vitals still recorded at 0/0.
Standing medications confirmed and ready for use. 80. Reassessments; retake vital signs: glucose; Partner: Confirmed blood glucose remains at 355 mg/dL.
pertinent past medical history; Note past insulin-dependent diabetes and no family allergies.
Trauma team called for possible DNR.
Patient passed to trauma team without complications.
All documentation and return plans coordinated.
Pulse rechecked and still undetectable.
Repositioning patient and checking line access for consistency.
EKG confirmed presence of initial asystole as time on scene.
No relevant information to add regarding patient's environment.
Airway still secured and no changes in airway condition.
No timeline for specific event leading to collapse.
Crew is stable. Hospital has regained patient pulse.
Diabetes still recorded as noted in history. 93. Reassessments; redo history of present illness: events leading up to the event; Paramedic: Minimal lore given by family as to prior condition.
Meds on board not yet used.
No EKG rhythm signal due to complex pulse.
EMS operations complete. Ambulance ready to dispatch again.
No descriptors available due to patient loss of communication.
Rescue diving deeper into examining IV.
No pulse palpable despite extensive probes.
Further anatomy assessments made where possible.
